## Page 284

### [p284_c0001] Federal Capital Projects Virtual Public Hearing
[p284_c0002] 
February 23, 2021
[p284_c0003] 
12 Online Comments
### [p284_c0004] Deborah Wright, Rosedale, NY
[p284_c0005] 
Long Island Railroad Projects - Locust Manor and Laurelton both stations need elevators -large senior population, disabilities, and families with strollers.
[p284_c0006] 
Maintenance issues- Rosedale and Laurelton stations need upkeep, stairs need repair, paint peeling and rust erosion on beams. Local stops being overlooked.
### [p284_c0007] Darian Jordan, Brooklyn, NY
[p284_c0008] 
Hello. my comment is in regards to the subways stations department. The closure of booths and eliminations of stations clerks proves to be a safety issue to the public. The rate of assaults and attacks on customers are high. With no clerks on the stations it will run the risk of an increase in these attacks. Posted on many booth windows are wanted signs of people who committed attacks or vandalisms. We can't ignore the idea of the potential threats that will occur if a station is desolate during off peak hours. The thought of you or a loved one being attacked on an empty station with no one to help is a scary reality. I personally have saved two lives last year from customers falling on the subway tracks. I at the time was on duty as a station booth clerk giving lunch reliefs from station to station. Because of the angle of the tracks, the train operator was unable to see the customers that fell onto the tracks near the third rail. Due to my previous training I was able to flag down and stop the train before they came into contact with the customer.
[p284_c0009] 
The major issue in the field is customers not paying their fare. This is a huge case in all boroughs and neighborhoods. From low income customers to business men and women evading the fare. A huge but simple improvement would be for the booth clerk to have control of locking and unlocking the service gate without the customers being able to override that by simply having someone push the release bar for them. We have to identify and understand the customers we are providing service to. New York City Transit customers are not the same as Long Island Railroad or Metro North Railroad riders. What works for those two divisions proves very ineffective for NYCT division. At the end of the day whether the fare is $2.75 or $13. Every single day thousands of customers do not pay the fare, many of which respond: "I don't feel like it." The crisis has become as if customers have more control over the system than the employees themselves. Payment of fare is not being enforced neither by police or policy. If we do not secure payment of fare we will always have a financial deficit. Now in the event of an emergency or a rapid evacuation as long as there is a booth clerk present, the booth clerk can unlock the service gate when required. During police responses whether for investigations or emergencies the booth clerk gives them access to stations in a quick timely manner. During this pandemic station closures period, there have been other issues that have occurred. Various departments ( infrastructure, signals, third rail, power with all their miscellaneous equipment, etc) all require entry into the system. Having booth clerks at all times with the improvement to customer fare control proves to be as asset and not a financial burden.
### [p284_c0010] Mr. X, Manhattan, NY
[p284_c0011] 
RE:2/23 PUBLIC HEARING ATTN:FOYE
[p284_c0012] 
THIS PUBLIC HEARING IS ANOTHER REASON WHY YOU SHOULD THROW IN THE TOWEL LIKE ARNOLD SKAALAND WHICH IS WHAT HE DID FOR ROBERT (BOB) BACKLUND.IT'S ALSO ANOTHER REASON WHY I FEEL YOU SHOULD STAY HOME AND BE A HOUSEWIFE.ALL YOU HAVE TO DO IS WEAR AN APRON AND A DRESS BECAUSE YOU'RE TOO BIG TO WEAR A MINISKIRT.I CAN TELL YOU A BEDTIME STORY BUT THE PROBLEM IS YOU'RE TOO BIG TO SIT ON MY LAP.ALTHOUGH IT'S SCHEDULED TO START AT 5 PM A TIME WHEN MOST PEOPLE ARE NORMALLY WORKING YOU DECIDED WE SHOULD REGISTER ON THE SAME DAY BEGINNING AT 10 AM.
[p284_c0013] 
SINCE 1/15/20 YOU HELD SEVERAL PUBLIC HEARINGS AND YOU NEVER MENTIONED THE MISSING ELEVATORS THAT SHOULD BE SERVING THE 2,4 AND 5 TRAINS AT HOSTOS COLLEGE ALTHOUGH WE ADVOCATED FOR THEM OVER 2 YEARS AGO.THAT SHOWS HOW CONCERNED YOU ARE ABOUT THE MINORITES AND POOR NEIGHBORHOODS.BEFORE 1/15 YOU WERE THE ONE WHO WENT AFTER ME AND ASKED THE MTAPD PARTICULARLY GODZILLA/IKE TURNER/RICHARD PRYOR/SHAFT/WILLIE DYNAMITE/WILTON (WILT) CHAMBERLAIN TO HELP YOU MOVE IN ON MY PARADE BECAUSE YOU FELT MY COMMENTS ABOUT YOUR PREDECESSOR WERE DISGUSTING ALTHOUGH YOU MADE A RACIST REMARK TO ANTHONY (TONY) UTANO.
[p284_c0014] 
HERE'S SOMETHING YOU AND SARAH CAN DO:YOU CAN ORDER 63 FOOT CARS FOR THE IRT DIVISION.THE TRAINS CAN CARRY 8 CARS.YOU CAN MAKE CONNECTIONS AT THE HOYT STREET STATIONS.YOU CAN MAKE CONNECTIONS AT THE BEDFORD PARK BOULEVARD AND FORDHAM ROAD STATIONS.YOU CAN MAKE CONNECTIONS AT QUEENS AND
[p284_c0015] 
_1_
[p284_c0016] 
_Master Page # 284 of 604 - Finance Committee Meeting 3/17/2021_

## Page 285
[p285_c0001] 
QUEENSBORO PLAZAS. YOU CAN MAKE CONNECTIONS AT THE 34TH STREET STATIONS. YOU CAN MAKE CONNECTIONS AT THE 42ND STREET STATIONS WEST FROM 5TH AVENUE. YOU CAN MAKE CONNECTIONS AT THE 50TH STREET STATIONS WEST FROM 5TH AVENUE. AT 50TH STREET/8TH AVENUE YOU CAN DISMIS THE LOWER LEVEL PLATFORMS. AT BERGEN/SMITH STREETS YOU CAN DO THE SAME THUS FIX THE EXPRESS TRACKS. ON ALL 4 TRACK LINES THE EXPRESS TRACKS MUST BE USED AT ALL TIMES.
### [p285_c0002] Arlyne Blitz, Brooklyn, NY
[p285_c0003] 
As an older resident of Dumbo, I have trouble climbing the 4 flights of steps -- two to get up to the turnstile area, and two more to get to the street. The closest accessible station is almost a mile away at Metrotech Center. That is too far to expect someone to walk just to talk public transportation. My job is in Manhattan; there are no buses that go over the bridge.
[p285_c0004] 
I understand that the policy is to make every second station accessible, but in most locations, as in Manhattan, stations are closer together and it is not a problem to use another station that is only 4-6 short blocks in either direction. In this area, that is not the case. We have only one way to walk to a subway -- the river is on the other side.
[p285_c0005] 
An additional problem is that this station not only serves workers, many tech workers, who come in for the day at the same time and flood the narrow platform, but the hundreds of visitors who come to Brooklyn Bridge Park most of the year. The park keeps expanding, and developing, and the waterfront restaurants have only exacerbated that problem. The influx of tourists was not anticipated when this was a small factory area. Many new residential and tech buildings have been built in the last ten years. The times have changed; the needs have changed; this has to be addressed.
[p285_c0006] 
Lastly, the entrance to the turnstiles is jammed every day. There are 3 turnstiles, columns in the way, the ticket booth, and vending machines all in an extremely small space. We need a larger turnstile area, and another means of egress, both for everyday use, and in case of emergency. Elevator accessibility needs to be at the York Street entrance.
[p285_c0007] 
I don't know if all these changes of the last 10-20 years have been properly appreciated for the transformation they have brought to the area.
[p285_c0008] 
This whole community has been asking for this upgrade for many years. Other areas seem to get priority over us. We ask you to please examine the current conditions here and add this station as a priority.
[p285_c0009] 
Thank you,
[p285_c0010] 
Arlyne Blitz
### [p285_c0011] Deborah Wright, Rosedale, NY
[p285_c0012] 
Elevator at Parson and Archer, E/J Subway lines, : The elevator is not transparent in comparison to subway elevators on the same train line at Kew Gardens and Forest Hills.. As is, it is a crime opportunity for those up to no good, that may victimize the elderly or other vulnerable persons . Also, a welcome haven to homeless squatters. Elevator needs to be changed.
[p285_c0013] 
Bus Design - right now with Covid-19 buses are not crowded , but future design should eliminate the steps, that lead to the back of the bus . As it causes Bottleneck in the front , during school and rush hours , Most people will not climb up the steps , to make room for others
### [p285_c0014] Aaron Kominos-Smith, Brooklyn, NY
[p285_c0015] 
Please rank single/center platform stations with a single entrance at one extremity in order of usage. These are the most dangerous stations in the system when crowded and require attention. The York Street station on the F line is one such station and is regularly overcrowded and safety is increasingly becoming an issue. There are no public plans for building a second entrance/exit to this station. It seems the MTA should produce proposals ASAP. The York St station services DUMBO, a growing community with multiple residential and commercial buildings under construction. The station is overcapacity during peak times and money must be allocated to propose an additional entrance/exit to the station. The NYPD has already demonstrated that it will not tolerate dangerous overcrowding on the platform. It has closed the station on multiple occasions for hours. The Station is a disaster waiting to happen and the MTA should act now so we don't regret it in the future. Thank you for your time.
[p285_c0016] 
_2_
[p285_c0017] 
_Master Page # 285 of 604 - Finance Committee Meeting 3/17/2021_

## Page 286

### [p286_c0001] Mayor Dennis Siry, Amityville, NY
[p286_c0002] 
The Village of Amityville wishes to express its enthusiastic support for the proposed ADA upgrades to the Amityville LIRR Station, in the upcoming capital budget, which include installation of a much-needed elevator. The improvement project would resolve long-standing issues in transportation access and ensure greater accessibility for elderly and disabled residents, commuters and all visitors using the station. The Village is embarking on a revitalization of its downtown and is adding several multi-family apartment complexes such as Village by the Bay, Avalon Bay and others, representing several hundreds of potential LIRR riders. We are also creating a pedestrian/bicycle path to increase connectivity between our downtown with the station. The intended station improvements will create an inviting and safe travel experience for all.
[p286_c0003] 
We ask that you approve the Amityville Station project as proposed and thank you for your service to our Village.
[p286_c0004] 
Sincerely,
[p286_c0005] 
Mayor Dennis M. Siry
### [p286_c0006] Sandy Galef, Ossing, NY
[p286_c0007] 
In the midst of this pandemic, we have radically changed how we interact with public transportation. As we look toward brighter days, it is important that our infrastructure is up to the demands it will face. This proposal is vitally important to meeting that future head on. These projects individually improve reliability, expand services, update systems, and enhance safe access to MTA services. Several of the projects focus on stations and improvement north of Westchester, bringing enhanced services to these commuters. The proposal will even pave the Cold Spring parking lot. Taken together, these capital projects will jump start our economy by putting thousands to work and providing much needed money for our workers. They will revitalize our transportation infrastructure for many years to come.
[p286_c0008] 
Through COVID-19 we have seen many avoid congested subways and trains, walking rather than taking the bus. But public transportation is not only for the fair-weather traveler. Our essential workers rely on public transportation to get to and from work, rain or shine. Without robust public transportation where would we be? Would our doctors and nurses, grocery store workers and teachers and so many more have gotten to work? Our system came through for us, providing safe passage as we performed our essential tasks. More than any individual project this proposal declares confidently that New York will redouble our efforts to provide public transportation as a hallmark of a vibrant state. Our system has been through a lot. This proposal ensures we will continue to meet our transportation needs into the future.
### [p286_c0009] Nicholas DeSantis, Brooklyn, NY
[p286_c0010] 
I have lived in DUMBO for 13 years and have seen first hand the challenges outlined and presented below.
[p286_c0011] 
The neighborhoods of DUMBO and Vinegar Hill are serviced mainly by the York Street F train station. The aging York Street station has a single entrance/exit and is not ADA compliant. At rush hour it has a line of commuters extending outside, onto Jay Street, waiting to enter the station to reach the turnstiles. At the same time it can have a line of commuters backed up from the turnstiles, down the ramp, to the platform trying to exit. This leads to unsafe congestion on what is already a narrow platform. There have been multiple occasions over the years that the station had to be shutdown for safety reason when DUMBO hosted an event or was the place to be for July 4th. All of these issues are well documented. Multiple news outlets have covered it. It has been raised by the DUMBO BID, by the DUMBO Action Committee, and many commuters. It has been brought to the attention of a number of local politicians as well.
[p286_c0012] 
https://www.wsj.com/articles/brooklyn-startups-face-speed-bumps-11576414800 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dP9_15hYGOs
[p286_c0013] 
Ridership for York Street has nearly doubled since 2011 and given the current development projects planned or underway for DUMBO and vicinity, the usage of the station will only increase.
[p286_c0014] 
York Street is the 19th busiest station in Brooklyn based on 2019 MTA statistics. Of those stations York street saw the largest percentage of ridership increase from 2018 and is the only one of the stations to show an increase in ridership every year since 2014.
[p286_c0015] 
From 2104-2019 York Street usage has risen 42.3%, ranking it 11th in growth for that period, but ranking it 4th in overall ridership within that same group.
[p286_c0016] 
York Street has ridership on par with the WTC, but far, far fewer turnstiles and exits.
[p286_c0017] 
----- (This from MTA data, but does not translate well in your comments tool)
[p286_c0018]

### [p287_c0010] Annual Subway Ridership

[p287_c0013] 
_Master Page # 287 of 604 - Finance Committee Meeting 3/17/2021_

## Page 288
[p288_c0001] 
Please Note: The ridership numbers for the station are also probably under recorded as the Emergency door is used by most riders that are exiting and therefore they are not counted by turnstiles. I estimate at least a 25% undercount since 2015 when the alarm on the door was silenced. Using this as context for a revised ranking, York Street would be the 4th busiest station in Brooklyn and the second busiest Brooklyn station that is not ADA compliant; Court Street being the first.
[p288_c0002] 
I see no specific line item on the 2021 Project list to address the needs of the DUMBO/Vinegar Hill communities with regards to the York Street Subway Station.
[p288_c0003] 
DUMBO needs:
- [p288_c0004] 1. Funds for a study for a new egress for York Street and/or to make it ADA compliant 2. Funds to provide an interim solution to some of the overcrowding by moving the turnstiles back from the entrance, and adding more turnstiles.
[p288_c0005] 
Nicholas DeSantis DUMBO Resident 13 years DAC Member
### [p288_c0006] Senator Pete Harckham, Peekskill, NY
[p288_c0007] 
Testimony New York State Senator Pete Harckham Capital Projects Virtual Public Hearing Federal Fiscal Year 2021 Feb. 23, 2021
[p288_c0008] 
Thank for holding this hearing on the MTA's Capital Program federal funding requests.
[p288_c0009] 
First, I want to take this opportunity to thank MTA employees for their hard work and sacrifice in order to keep the trains and buses running during the pandemic. We can never forget that some of these workers made the ultimate sacrifice to keep vital public transit lifelines open. Public transportation remains of vital importance to the essential workers that are keeping us safe. In addition, sound transit infrastructure will be badly needed as we recover from the pandemic.
[p288_c0010] 
So it is important that MTA and Metro-North have prioritized state of good repair in their request for federal funding. Not only will these projects address aging Metro-North track, signals, and stations, they will also generate hundreds of jobs that will have a positive ripple effect throughout our regional economy. The MTA must ensure that these projects are awarded promptly so they can have a maximum effect in helping to revitalize our economy.
[p288_c0011] 
In closing, I once again urge the MTA and Metro-North to address the issue of safety at grade crossings. Earlier this month we marked the sixth anniversary of the deadly Valhalla grade crossing accident. It is unacceptable that the crossing remains the most dangerous on the system. The MTA must work in conjunction with the local communities, other state agencies and the federal government to eliminate grade crossings.
[p288_c0012] 
Thank You
### [p288_c0013] Charlton D'Souza, Queens Village, NY
[p288_c0014] 
The Long Island rail road Hollis station must be rebuilt in this capital program. The platforms are collapsing because of poor conditions. The platform is held up by wood beams
### [p288_c0015] Peter Beadle, Rego Park, NY
[p288_c0016] 
Dear Members of the Board;
[p288_c0017] 
Queens Community Board 6, covering Forest Hills and Rego Park, through which the LIRR mainline and the E, F, M and R subway lines run, requests that the MTA's capital projects budget include funding for the installation of ADA accessible elevators at each of our LIRR and subway stations. The ability for those with mobility challenges to live a full and active life is made significantly more difficult when our transportation systems are not ADA compliant. These elevators are essential and long overdue.
[p288_c0018] 
Thank you, Peter W. Beadle Second Vice Chair, CB6 Queens Co-Chair Transportation Committee
[p288_c0019] 
_5_
[p288_c0020] 
_Master Page # 288 of 604 - Finance Committee Meeting 3/17/2021_

Built from public NYC/MTA government records. Every structured record cites evidence blocks in official source documents. Data was extracted with an LLM pipeline and may contain errors — verify against the cited sources.
